nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; generally found from the surface to about 20–30 m in coral-rich lagoons and semi-protected seaward reefs, closely associated with live Pocillopora and other small-polyped stony corals on which it feeds

decorPreferences: live rock with numerous hiding places
abundant living small-polyped stony corals (Pocillopora) as a food source

diet.notes: Obligate corallivore. In the wild, has been documented to feed almost exclusively on live coral polyps, particularly of the genus Pocillopora and other small-polyped stony corals. In captivity, this specialised diet is the defining husbandry challenge: most individuals refuse all prepared and frozen foods and slowly starve unless provided with a continuous supply of living coral colonies. A few specimens have been reported to accept enriched frozen preparations (mysid shrimp, soaked formulas, sponge-based foods), but this is the exception rather than the rule. Generally regarded as not suitable for the home aquarium for this reason.

avoidWith: conspecifics outside a bonded male-female pair

imageCandidates.notes: Francois Libert; 1000x667. Tack-sharp full lateral view of a vibrant adult clearly showing blue pinstripes on pale body, yellow lower body, orange snout, black eye bar and orange-red caudal peduncle - textbook diagnostic shot.
Philippe Bourjon; 1200x899. Sharp, vivid in-situ pair on Acropora coral, illustrating the species' monogamous pairing and coral-associated habitat; all diagnostic markings visible.
Rainer Kretzberg; 640x506. Vivid, sharp single individual at a head-down feeding angle over coral; good colour and pattern, modest resolution.

summary: An Indian Ocean butterflyfish with a pale body crossed by fine dark pinstripes and a yellow-and-orange posterior. An obligate coral-polyp feeder, it is generally considered expert-only and unsuitable for most home aquariums.
careNotes:
The Melon Butterflyfish (Chaetodon trifasciatus) is widely regarded as one of the most demanding butterflyfish to maintain in captivity, and is generally considered unsuitable for the home aquarium. The reason is dietary: in the wild the species is an obligate corallivore, feeding almost exclusively on the live polyps of Pocillopora and other small-polyped stony corals. Most captive specimens refuse prepared and frozen foods entirely and slowly starve without a continuous supply of living coral colonies. A small number of individuals have been reported to eventually accept enriched mysis shrimp, sponge-based preparations, or coral-based formulas, but this cannot be relied upon and should not be assumed when acquiring the fish.

In the wild, adults are generally understood to form lifelong monogamous pairs, swimming and feeding together over a defended territory on the reef. The species is reported as territorial and aggressive toward conspecifics outside the bonded pair, and may also harass other butterflyfish in a shared aquarium. If a bonded pair is attempted, both fish should be introduced simultaneously to reduce conflict.

As an obligate coral feeder, the Melon Butterflyfish will consume live SPS corals (small-polyped stony corals), Tridacna clams, tubeworms, and anemones — it is not considered reef-safe in any practical sense. Juveniles tend to shelter secretively within coral structures before adopting the more open, pair-bonded behaviour of adults.

additional.notes: Used for taxonomy confirmation (subgenus Corallochaetodon, described M. Park 1797), monogamous adult pair-bonding, territorial behaviour toward other Chaetodon, secretive juvenile phase within coral structures, and Indian-Ocean range (East Africa to Western Java).
